headline: Butte Miners Launch General Strike, 1919
body: On February 8, 1919, the copper miners of Butte, Montana, launched a general strike in response to a proposed dollar-per-day wage cut by mine owners. This action ignited a fierce labor dispute in a city already renowned for its militant unionism and a history of industrial conflict. Butte, often called the 'Richest Hill on Earth' due to its vast mineral wealth, was a crucible of the American labor movement. The miners, many of whom were members of the Industrial Workers of the World (IWW) or other radical labor organizations, were determined to resist any attempts to diminish their hard-won wages and working conditions. The strike quickly paralyzed the city's vital copper production, drawing national attention to the ongoing struggle between powerful mining corporations and organized labor. This event was not an isolated incident but rather a continuation of Butte's long and often violent labor history, characterized by clashes over wages, safety, and union recognition. The 1919 strike underscored the deep-seated tensions and the unwavering resolve of Montana's working class to fight for economic justice and fair treatment in the face of formidable industrial power.
